title: `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ping_group_range` not accessible in rootless container
body:
### Issue Description

I can't get volume mounts to be remapped to the container UID. Since the ways to do that, using `uidmap` seem to interfere with container creation. I've read earlier issues but that didn't help me in fixing this.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Pull a container image with `uid` and `gid` 10001. (I doubt this is relevant, and I tried both with and without one. Using `alpine` below.)
2. Create a container out of it, with a volume mount (files owned by 501:1000).
```console 
$ podman \
    run \
     --entrypoint=sh \
     --uidmap=10001:501:1 \
     --gidmap=10001:1000:1 \
     --uidmap=0:0:1 \
     --gidmap=0:0:1 \
     --sysctl='net.ipv4.ping_group_range=500 10002' \
     --volume="$(pwd):$(pwd)" \
     --workdir="$(pwd)" \
     --user=10001 \
     --rm \
     -it \
     alpine \
       -c 'id'
Error: OCI runtime error: crun: write to `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ping_group_range`: Invalid argument
```

Tried, to no avail:

1. `--user=10001` or not.
2. Various  `--sysctl='net.ipv4.ping_group_range=...'` variants.
3. Various `uidmap` and `gidmap` values.
4. Leaving out the last `gidmap`.
5. Various images.
6. Editing `/etc/containers/containers.conf` inside the VM, to add:

    ```ini
    [containers]
      default_sysctls = ["net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 0"]
    ```

### Describe the results you received

Error: OCI runtime error: crun: write to `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ping_group_range`: Invalid argument

### Describe the results you expected

Normal operation of the container.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

I've been testing both on the remote end (MacOS) as well as inside the VM.

### Additional information

```console
$  podman machine init --cpus 4 --memory 8192 --volume "$HOME:$HOME" --timezone utc --now
Extracting compressed file: podman-machine-default_fedora-coreos-39.20231204.2.1-qemu.aarch64.qcow2: done  
Image resized.
Machine init complete
Starting machine "podman-machine-default"
Waiting for VM ...
Mounting volume... /Users/dummy:/Users/dummy
```

`"$(pwd)"` is a descendant of $HOME.

comments.body: that is expected, the specified groups are not mapped inside the user namespace.  You need something like `--gidmap=500:1000:9503`

@giuseppe I can't follow. https://docs.podman.io/en/v4.4/markdown/options/gidmap.container.html states `gidmap=container_gid:host_gid:amount`. 10001 is the container's uid and id, and on my macOS host my uid is 501 and primary gid 20. How did you arrive at `500:1000:9503`?

@giuseppe

The fragment of your instruction doesn't work for me either:

```console
$ podman run --mount type=bind,source="$(pwd)",target=/builds --uidmap=10001:501:1 --gidmap=500:1000:9503 --entrypoint=sh --rm -it alpine
Error: preparing container 12f77168442abb6bd50cdde952f5a315dde7b735fa360877840cf61fbc8e85c7 for attach: mounting storage for container 12f77168442abb6bd50cdde952f5a315dde7b735fa360877840cf61fbc8e85c7: container ID 0 cannot be mapped to a host ID
```

you are using `--gidmap=0:0:1 --gidmap=10001:1000:1`, so the gids available inside the user namespace are 0 and 10001.

The kernel complains when you pass something like `--sysctl='net.ipv4.ping_group_range=500 10002' \` because the group 500 is not mapped inside the user namespace:

```
$ podman  run  --uidmap=10001:501:1 --gidmap=10001:1000:1  --uidmap=0:0:1 --gidmap=0:0:1   --sysctl='net.ipv4.ping_group_range=500 10002' --user 0:0 --rm -it alpine id
Error: OCI runtime error: crun: write to `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ping_group_range`: Invalid argument

$ podman  run  --uidmap=10001:501:1   --gidmap=10001:1000:1 --uidmap=0:0:1 --gidmap=0:0:1 --sysctl='net.ipv4.ping_group_range=10001 10001' --user 0:0 --rm -it alpine id
uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root)
```
Why don't the later examples work, e.g. https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/21031#issuecomment-1900512821, that don't set the sysctl?

Okay, I fixed it with `--uidmap=0:0:1` `--gidmap=0:0:1`. So, all mapped uids and gids need to exist inside the container. The range is not a passive configuration option, so to say. And any uid and gid that exists in the container (how is this determined, `/etc/passwd`?) must be mapped if any mapping option was set on the host side. So it's a bijective mapping. I hope this information will be documented somewhere since to me that's surprising. For example, one can run a process with a fictional uid and gid, but this this doesn't translate to this type of mapping.
